nativeRange.habitat: Found in small, isolated forest pools and slow-moving tributary streams in the Río Calima headwaters. The water is soft and tannin-stained — what hobbyists call blackwater — with dense riparian vegetation and accumulations of leaf litter along the banks and bottom.

decorPreferences: dense fine-leaved planting (Java moss, Cabomba, hornwort)
dark substrate (fine sand or dark gravel)
driftwood and dried leaf litter for tannins and cover
open swimming space in the centre with planted margins

diet.notes: Accepts high-quality micro-pellets, crushed flake, and granules as a staple. Small live and frozen foods are taken eagerly — bloodworm (chironomid larvae), Daphnia, Artemia (brine shrimp), mosquito larvae, and tubifex are all suitable. A varied diet that includes live or frozen items is important for maintaining vibrant coloration and conditioning fish that are intended for breeding.

avoidWith: slow-moving or long-finned fish susceptible to fin-nipping
very small nano fish or fry
boisterous or aggressive species that would outcompete them for food

summary: The Rainbow Tetra (Nematobrycon lacortei) is a seldom-seen Colombian tetra closely related to the Emperor Tetra. Males display vivid red irises and a blue-violet body with scattered red, blue, and green iridescence; females have bluish-green eyes. It is best kept in groups of eight or more in soft, acidic blackwater conditions.
careNotes:
The Rainbow Tetra is an uncommon fish in the aquarium hobby, typically available only as wild-caught imports from its restricted native range in the Río San Juan drainage of western Colombia — specifically the Río Calima tributary, within the Choco and Valle de Cauca departments. It is closely related to the Emperor Tetra (Nematobrycon palmeri) and shares the characteristic elongated extension of the middle caudal-fin rays seen in males of both species. The most reliable way to tell the two apart is eye colour: Rainbow Tetra males have a bright red iris, while Emperor Tetra males have a blue one. Beyond that, N. lacortei displays more saturated blue-violet colouration along the back, scattered red, blue, and green iridescence across the body and fins, a shorter and more triangular dorsal fin in males (rather than the sickle shape seen in N. palmeri), and one fewer pectoral-fin ray. Females have bluish-green eyes and a noticeably fuller body, making it possible to sex the fish even as juveniles.

Water quality is the most critical care factor. Soft, acidic water — pH 5.0–7.0 and gH 1–8 — that closely replicates the species' blackwater home environment is strongly recommended. Blackwater refers to the tea-coloured, tannin-rich water typical of South American forest streams, where decomposing leaf litter lowers pH and softens the water naturally. Harder or more alkaline conditions are tolerated in the short term but tend to result in faded colour and reduced vitality over time. Adding dried Indian almond leaves (Terminalia catappa) or using peat filtration to introduce mild tannin staining is generally beneficial. Temperature should be maintained between 22 and 28°C.

A group of at least 8–10 individuals is recommended to distribute the intermale posturing that males naturally display and to produce a cohesive shoaling effect. Males may spread their fins and spar with one another but rarely cause injury in a suitably sized aquarium. An 80-litre tank with at least an 80 cm footprint is the practical minimum for a small shoal; 100–120 L is preferable for a larger group or a community setup. Dense planting along the tank margins with open midwater swimming space in the centre replicates the natural habitat well.
breedingNotes: Breeding is achievable in a well-conditioned species tank but is considered moderately challenging. The main hurdles are the requirement for very soft, acidic water (pH 5.0–6.5, TDS below 100 ppm) and the tendency of adults to consume their own eggs and fry if left in the tank after spawning. To prepare fish for breeding, condition a pair or small group with live and frozen foods for two to four weeks, then move them to a dedicated 20–40 L breeding tank furnished with fine-leaved plants such as Java moss or Cabomba, or with spawning mops. The Rainbow Tetra is an egg scatterer that deposits eggs among the plants, with spawning typically occurring in the morning. Adults should be removed promptly after spawning is complete. The eggs are sensitive to light — the breeding tank should be kept in subdued light or darkness until the fry become free-swimming. Hatching occurs within 24–36 hours; fry are free-swimming approximately four to five days after that. First foods should be infusoria or a commercial fry food of equivalent fine particle size, progressing to microworms and Artemia nauplii (newly hatched brine shrimp) within the first week.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish — Nematobrycon lacortei
primary.notes: Primary source. Provided native range (Río Calima, Río San Juan drainage, Colombia), temperature 20–28°C, pH 5.0–7.2, hardness 18–179 ppm, minimum tank dimensions 120×30×30 cm, diet (live/frozen micro-foods plus dried), temperament, tankmates, and breeding notes (hatching 24–36 h, free-swimming day 4–5, infusoria first food).

additional.name: FishBase — Nematobrycon lacortei
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (3.6 cm SL), temperature 23–27°C, pH 5.6–7.2, hardness up to 12 dH, native range (San Juan River basin, South America), taxonomy (Characiformes), IUCN Data Deficient status (2014), and trophic level 3.0.

additional.name: TFH Magazine — All Hail the Emperor Tetras
additional.notes: Provided species-level morphological detail for N. lacortei: male red iris vs female greenish-blue iris, deep-purple dorsal colouration in males, dark-blue-to-black lateral stripe with scattered reds/blues/yellows on body and fins, male size approximately 1¾ in (4.5 cm). Confirmed N. lacortei is considerably smaller and more vividly coloured than N. palmeri.

additional.name: Tropical Fish Keeping — Rainbow Tetra (Nematobrycon lacortei)
additional.notes: Confirmed species is rarely seen in the aquarium hobby and typically available only as wild-caught specimens. Provided temperature 73–80°F (23–27°C), pH 5.6–7.2, hardness 1–12°H, lifespan 4–6 years, minimum 20-gallon tank, and distinguishing features (red-banded eyes, triangular dorsal fin vs sickle-shaped in Emperor Tetra).

additional.name: Aquadiction — Rainbow Tetra Species Spotlight
additional.notes: Confirmed native range (Río Calima, Río San Juan, western Colombia — Choco and Valle de Cauca), adult size up to 4 cm, lifespan up to 5 years, pH 5.0–7.0, gH 5–8, temperature 22–26°C, minimum group size 8+, and breeding notes (egg scatterer, dim light required, 24–36 h hatching, fry free-swimming day 4–5).

fish.breedingNotes: The Rainbow Tetra is an egg scatterer that deposits eggs among fine-leaved plants. Breeding requires very soft, acidic water (pH 5.0–6.5) and adults conditioned on live and frozen foods. Adults will consume their own eggs and should be removed promptly after spawning is complete. The eggs are sensitive to light, so the breeding tank should be kept in subdued light or darkness until the fry become free-swimming. Hatching occurs within 24–36 hours; fry are free-swimming by day four or five. First foods should be infusoria or an equivalent fine-particle commercial fry food, progressing to microworms and Artemia nauplii as the fry grow.
